items.title: ESA outlines high-tech lander instruments for 2050 Enceladus
items.summary: Saturn's moon Enceladus has become a prime solar system target for astrobiologists. This is because the small moon, which is just over 10% the diameter of Earth's moon, harbors a vast subsurface ocean beneath its icy crust. This subsurface ocean, combined with the geysers at Enceladus&#039

items.title: Orbit overload could devastate astronomy if 1.7 million proposed satellites brighten night sky
items.summary: A new European Southern Observatory (ESO) study has found that current proposals to launch more than 1.7 million satellites into orbit, including extremely bright ones, would have "devastating consequences for astronomy." According to the study, no more than 100,000 faint satellites, below naked-eye

items.title: How a giant planet survived its star's death, then migrated inward
items.summary: When astronomers discovered a giant planet orbiting a dead star in 2020, they wondered how it survived its star's violent demise. Now, observations from NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) may finally explain the planet's unlikely escape from destruction.

items.title: Urokodia! 518-million-year-old fossil shows beginning of spider's bite
items.summary: The earliest evidence of spiders' fangs has been identified in a 518-million-year-old fossil by scientists at the University of Leicester and Yunnan University.

items.title: Algae may have launched coral reefs by hijacking coral cells, genetic experiments suggest
items.summary: The reefs scattered throughout the tropics arose only after algae took up full-time residence in coral cells, supplying corals with abundant food and enabling them to build extensive shallow-water communities. But with warming oceans, algae are often abandoning coral—causing what's known as ble

items.title: NASA’s Webb Studies How Planet Survived Death of its Star
items.summary: NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope is giving us new insight into the far-future of solar systems like our own, as the agency continues to reveal the secrets of the universe and our place in it. Billions of years ago, a Sun-like star nearing the end of its life swelled tremendously in size to become a

items.title: From fields to space farming, new tool detects crop drought stress before it's visible

items.title: Perfluorooctanoic acid in the Seto Inland Sea: Variability, transport, and fate
items.summary: Perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA) is one of the per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), a class of substances within a broader universe of organofluorine compounds. PFOA has potential adverse effects on human health and environmental safety because of its toxicity and bioaccumulation. Its innate che

items.title: Black-box optimization weather intervention method supports future disaster mitigation
items.summary: In recent years, the frequency of weather-related natural disasters—cyclones, torrential rains, floods—has increased as a consequence of global warming. These disasters cause billions of dollars in damage and losses every year. As a result, there is great interest in weather control, the process by

items.title: Video: Thousands of planets are hidden in this image
items.summary: Millions of stars. Thousands of hidden worlds. One unprecedented view of our galaxy. Three years since launch, ESA's Euclid space telescope reveals the center of the Milky Way galaxy in extraordinary detail: a mosaic of tens of millions of stars captured in just 26 hours. items.title: Woodcock charge deer to defend nests, footage reveals
items.summary: American woodcock, short, plump shorebirds with long, thin beaks, are widely known for their bobbing stride and nasally "peent" calls, but not for being aggressive. Yet one April afternoon, when a deer sniffed around a woodcock hen's ground nest looking for food, the hen lunged at it, scaring i

items.title: Caltech Welcomes Astrophysicist Ray Jayawardhana as New President
items.summary: Ray Jayawardhana begins his tenure today as the 10th president of the California Institute of Technology. His selection as Caltech’s president, and as the Sonja and William Davidow Presidential Chair and professor of astronomy, was announced Jan. 6. Jayawardhana succeeds Thomas Rosenbaum, who had se

items.title: 400-year-old painting reveals a bat's secret diet

items.title: Are you 'happee' or are you 'happeh?' Study links accents to social classes
items.summary: Our accents give away more about social class than we think, according to new research led by Lancaster University. The new sociolinguistic study focusing on Mancunian accents, published in the journal, Language Variation and Change, shows that the final vowel in words like happy, baby, chilly and c

items.title: Mismatched work–life boundaries while working from home can push couples toward breaking up
items.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic transformed the way people work, making remote and work-from-home (WFH) jobs far more common than ever before. Even after social distancing ended, many companies and employees chose to stick with this model because it offers greater flexibility. People can work from where they

items.title: What made trees possible? New research points to drought
items.summary: A study is reframing a fundamental question in plant evolution: What made trees possible? items.title: Were Clovis foragers in Late Pleistocene North America big-game hunters, or just big-game scavengers?
items.summary: There are currently 15 well-documented Late Pleistocene localities in North America in which Clovis points are found associated with proboscidean remains (of mammoth, mastodon and gomphothere). Archaeologists routinely assume these localities represent evidence that Clovis people hunted these multi-

items.title: Why taking a sick day depends on more than being sick
items.summary: As winter illness spreads and households face cost-of-living pressure, many Australians cannot treat a sick day as a simple health decision. items.title: Webb reveals merger scars in galaxies that stopped forming stars 9 billion years ago
items.summary: Research has shed new light on why some distant galaxies suddenly stop forming stars. An international team led by astronomers at the University of Nottingham has used the James Webb Space Telescope to study a large sample of recently "quenched" galaxies in the distant universe, observed around nine

items.title: NASA’s TESS Mission Finds Planetary System in New Way
items.summary: For the first time, NASA’s TESS (Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite) mission has identified a planet orbiting a distant star thanks to ripples in space-time. Unlike the star-hugging transiting planets TESS regularly reveals, the newfound world is a super-Jupiter orbiting far from its host star. “

items.title: NASA testirala AI satelit koji sam prepoznaje što vidi iz svemira
items.summary: SATELIT YAM-9, opremljen NASA-inom AI tehnologijom, može samostalno identificirati objekte iz orbite i primati naredbe na prirodnom jeziku, što revolucionizira analizu satelitskih snimaka.

items.title: Helping children navigate the emotional aftershocks of an earthquake
items.summary: The devastating earthquake in Venezuela has left countless families grieving, displaced and searching for loved ones. For many South Florida families with ties to the region, the tragedy feels especially close to home.

items.title: Galaxy groups hiding in the universe's emptiest places
items.summary: Imagine standing in the emptiest place the universe has to offer, a stretch of cosmic ocean so vast that light takes tens of millions of years to cross it, and yet still finding company. items.title: Darwin residents want answers on toxic gas emissions: Science shows their concerns are warranted
items.summary: For years, Darwin residents have been concerned about toxic chemicals emitted by the city's two gas export facilities. In fact, some locals are so worried about these airborne chemicals—particularly benzene—they recently formed a community group and launched a fundraiser to buy a regulation-gra

items.title: Himalayan pangolin emerges as distinct species, confirmed with DNA from 19th-century specimen
items.summary: The pangolin is a midsize mammal found only in Africa and Asia. The pangolins' scales make them unique, but these scales have become their undoing. Pangolins are poached for their scales, making them the most highly trafficked mammals in the world and, consequently, at high risk of extinction.

items.title: Polymer network reconfigures in sequence, helping elastomers stay tough under strain
items.summary: Shock-absorbing sneaker soles are likely made of polyurethane, a highly elastic and tough polymer. The ability of these elastomers to absorb impact without breaking is extremely important for practical applications. While multiple strategies exist for enhancing elastomer toughness, each has its limi

items.title: Srušen apsolutni rekord za lipanj, svjetski oceani nikad topliji
items.summary: SVJETSKI oceani srušili su temperaturni rekord za lipanj, dosegnuvši 20,86°C. Uzrok su El Niño i klimatske promjene, a stručnjaci upozoravaju na moguće ekstremno vrijeme i ugrozu morskog života.

items.title: U Portugalu pronađen jedan od najotrovnijih pauka na svijetu. Može izazvati nekrozu
items.summary: U PORTU je otkrivena nova vrsta otrovnog pauka, čileanski pauk samac (Loxosceles laeta). Iako mu ugriz može uzrokovati nekrozu, istraživači kažu da je rizik za ljude nizak zbog njegove povučenosti.

items.title: Dabrovi u Londonu spriječili poplave na željezničkoj postaji
items.summary: KOLONIJA dabrova vraćena u zapadni London uspješno je spriječila poplave na obližnjoj postaji. Njihove brane usporavaju vodu, a njihov povratak je obogatio i lokalnu bioraznolikost.

items.title: NASA rolls out three robotic moon missions as 2029 lunar base plans take shape
items.summary: NASA on Tuesday announced new uncrewed missions to aid in the future creation of a lunar surface base, a project beginning to take shape despite recent setbacks.

items.title: World's oceans break June heat record: EU monitor
items.summary: The world's oceans just experienced their hottest June on record and could set fresh highs in the months ahead as El Nino and climate change drive temperatures even higher, scientists said Wednesday.

items.title: NASA vows to send ball to the moon if US wins World Cup
items.summary: NASA's chief on Tuesday tried to rally Team U.S. players in this year's World Cup tournament, promising to send a soccer ball to the moon if they win it all.

items.title: Italy displays paintings from an ancient Etruscan tomb, its latest cultural acquisition
items.summary: Italy on Tuesday put on display one of the best known examples of Etruscan painting, panels from a tomb that it acquired for 15 million euros ($17 million) in the Culture Ministry's buying spree of big-ticket pieces of the country's cultural heritage.

items.title: Znanstvenici: Čini se da jajnici razvijaju drugu ulogu nakon menopauze
items.summary: NOVA studija na miševima sugerira da jajnici nakon menopauze nisu neaktivni. Umjesto toga, preuzimaju novu, imunosnu ulogu, što osporava dosadašnje pretpostavke i otvara nova pitanja o starenju.

items.title: NASA ulaže 30 milijardi dolara u izgradnju baze na Mjesecu
items.summary: NASA ubrzava planove za izgradnju baze na Mjesecu vrijedne 30 mlrd. $. Ugovori s privatnim tvrtkama za dostavu tereta dio su prve faze projekta, unatoč preprekama i utrci s Kinom.

items.title: Otkriven misterij Krvavih slapova na Antarktici
items.summary: KRVAVI slapovi na Antarktici fenomen su istjecanja slane vode bogate željezom, zarobljene ispod ledenjaka milijunima godina. U njoj žive jedinstvene bakterije, a nova studija otkrila je pulsiranje ledenjaka.

items.title: Physicists and AI model Claude 'collaborate' to prove a 10-year-old jamming conjecture
items.summary: A mathematical problem that had remained unsolved for more than 10 years in the physics of complex systems has finally been resolved through an unusual collaboration: one involving two theoretical physicists and an artificial intelligence system. items.title: AI-based demand forecasting creates planning reliability in the textile industry
items.summary: How can sales figures be forecast more reliably, production capacities planned fully digitally, and employee know-how systematically integrated at the same time? items.title: New modeling shows where to focus conservation efforts for Australia's endangered alpine ash
items.summary: An alpine ash forest is a sight to behold. Alpine ash (Eucalyptus delegatensis) is a tall eucalypt species that grows in the cool, wet mountains of southeastern Australia.

items.title: Cosmic eruption caught in the act by submillimeter array's new fastest response system
items.summary: On Jan. 26, 2026, the Submillimeter Array (SMA) on Maunakea crossed an important threshold for time-domain astronomy. items.title: Abundant catalyst converts methane into valuable liquid chemicals
items.summary: Scientists at the U.S. Department of Energy's (DOE) Brookhaven National Laboratory and their collaborators have demonstrated a promising new approach for converting methane—the primary component of natural gas—into liquid chemicals that are precursors for many industrial chemicals and fuels. items.title: Grasslands could lose four times more carbon uptake under future drought conditions
items.summary: The effects of individual climate factors on ecosystems are usually considered in isolation. However, in reality, they occur simultaneously and influence each other. Increasing CO2 concentrations in the atmosphere contribute to climate warming, which in turn fosters more frequent and intense drought

items.title: Shark‑spotting drones are about reassurance—not full protection
items.summary: Sharks are front of mind for many Sydneysiders and coastal New South Wales residents. In January, a teenager died amid a spate of attacks in and around Sydney. This month, a woman was bitten by a large great white while swimming close to shore and between the flags at Coogee Beach.

items.title: Loss of DNA protector gene exposes vulnerabilities in cancerous cells
items.summary: Every time a cell copies its DNA, parts of the genome are exposed and vulnerable to damage or errors. Molecular biologist Simon Boulton is interested in how cells spot and repair damage to their DNA, and what happens if this process fails or if key genes are mutated.

items.title: Quantum computer simulates hadronization, reproducing string breaking with 104 qubits
items.summary: By remotely accessing an IBM quantum computer, a research scientist at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory has successfully simulated a key process in particle physics: hadronization. Although based on a simplified model of quantum mechanics, the project lays the groundwork for how physicists can

items.title: 3,000-year-old Irish Bronze Age site may be one of Europe's earliest 'town-like' settlements
items.summary: A major prehistoric center in Ireland was among the first large, organized settlements to develop in Western Europe more than 3,000 years ago, new research reveals. The study, published today in Antiquity, identifies Haughey's Fort, near Armagh in Northern Ireland, as the focal point of a vast

items.title: Earth microbes can survive individual martian hazards—and evade astronaut immune systems
items.summary: Hopefully, we're about to travel back to the moon relatively soon. And while the original "giant leap for mankind" was taken by a human, Neil Armstrong brought a plethora of other forms of life along with him. Humans themselves are essentially walking ecosystems, and understanding how our micro

items.title: Tens of millions swelter as heat wave blasts US
items.summary: Tens of millions of Americans sweltered under furnace-like temperatures Tuesday as central and eastern cities hunkered down for a heat wave set to last through the July 4 holiday weekend.

items.title: Black locust deploys peptides to steer root bacteria into nitrogen fixation
items.summary: Plants need nitrogen to grow. Many legumes meet this need through a symbiotic relationship: They harbor bacteria that fix atmospheric nitrogen and make it available to the plant. items.summary: In celebration of the 250th birthday of the United States, NASA has unveiled four cosmic images from its Chandra X-ray Observatory, rendered in red, white and blue, that represent the wonders of the universe the agency explores. items.title: Astronomers find an enigmatic source that is most likely a Little Red Dot in formation
items.summary: Astronomers have, for the first time, found a source in the process of becoming a Little Red Dot, using the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST). items.title: Bacteria use linked motors to reel in resistance DNA with extreme force
items.summary: Every year, bacteria kill more than a million people worldwide through infections that no longer respond to antibiotics. In many cases, why those bacteria are so hard to stop comes down to their uniquely powerful structure.

items.title: Warming can shift freshwater crustaceans to a 'greener' diet
items.summary: Climate change is not only warming our lakes and rivers, it is also changing what invasive species eat. A new experimental study published in Limnology and Oceanography Letters shows that temperature-driven diet shifts in an invasive crustacean could alter its ecological role in freshwater food webs

items.title: Newly discovered corn trait may help improve crop drought tolerance

items.title: Layered ZnPS₃ emits single photons, opening new path for quantum chips
items.summary: Scientists from the Faculty of Physics at the University of Warsaw, in collaboration with teams from the National University of Singapore and Radboud University in the Netherlands, have observed single-photon emission from layered two-dimensional material ZnPS₃. items.title: Should AI chatbots simulate care for students? Alberta teachers say no
items.summary: Should schools allow AI systems that don't just answer students, but appear to care for them?

items.title: Super-deep diamond discovery may rewrite Earth's role in preserving the building blocks of life
items.summary: Two diamonds formed 700 kilometers below the Earth's surface reveal a life-giving synchronicity between shifting continents and the cycling of phosphorus, a vital building block of DNA and cell membranes.

items.summary: Isoprene is a volatile organic compound (VOC) that is produced naturally by plants. items.title: Mice actively seek better views to make visual decisions, virtual reality experiments show
items.summary: Animals don't experience the world passively. A hawk tilts its head to track prey. A person leans forward to read a sign. items.title: Data suggest greater glacial flood risk faced by Bhutan
items.summary: Researchers at Newcastle University have carried out the first comprehensive modeling of glacial lake outburst flood (GLOF) risk in Bhutan and identified previously unrecognized high-risk lakes.

items.title: Sound waves reconstruct Alaska fireball path after cameras miss key details
items.summary: When a bright fireball streaked across the Alaska sky last spring, the usual tools scientists rely on to track such events—cameras and satellites—did not provide a detailed picture. items.title: Prehistoric plague could have caused population collapse in Stone Age Europe
items.summary: Did a major epidemic of plague trigger a prolonged collapse in Europe's population in late Neolithic times—from around 5,600 to 4,000 years ago?

items.summary: Flight testing is a team sport. items.title: East Coast broccoli lowers costs and risks from California drought
items.summary: The burgeoning East Coast broccoli industry is helping lower costs slightly and reduce reliance on the drought-prone West. items.title: 'Atomic zoom' brings gum disease bacteria into sharp focus
items.summary: The technology at the center of the growing "resolution revolution" has again shown its value to scientists at Yale by revealing the secrets of gum disease.

items.title: Why some wolves react more strongly to trespassers: Breeders may hold key to scent-based barriers
items.summary: Wolves use their urine to communicate with each other. A recent study looked at the reactions of a pack to the marking of an intruder. This is a first step toward understanding what attracts or repels canids.

items.title: Legalized online sports betting drives fuel addiction's rise, study finds
items.summary: Since the Supreme Court's 2018 invalidation of the Professional and Amateur Sports Protection Act (PASPA), dozens of states have legalized sports betting.

items.title: Industrial-era pollution and warming reshape Tibetan lake after 1,000 years of climate swings
items.summary: The Tibetan Plateau, together with the Hindu Kush–Karakorum–Himalaya region, has more snow and ice than any other region on Earth apart from the polar regions. As a result, this high-altitude region is particularly sensitive to climate change, making it especially important in analyzing its impacts.

items.title: PFAS in most medicines can be replaced with alternatives
items.summary: Certain medicines contain per- and polyfluorinated alkyl compounds, known as PFAS, which are causing increasing environmental harm because of their long-lasting effects. A study published in Sustainable Chemistry and Pharmacy by the University of Freiburg, commissioned by the German Environment Agen

items.title: Computer scientists develop a new AI tool that rivals AlphaFold 3 in mapping RNA
items.summary: The same family of artificial intelligence that powers today's image generators is now being aimed at one of biology's hardest puzzles: the ever-changing, three-dimensional shapes of RNA. These are the molecules behind mRNA vaccines like the ones that prevent serious COVID-19 cases.

items.title: How a sugar building block influences viral attachment
items.summary: Sialic acid is a natural sugar building block found on the surfaces of our cells. It acts as a protective and recognition molecule and plays a central role in the development of the nervous system. Following the modular principle, individual components can be swapped for new building blocks, thereby

items.title: AI-powered platform lays the foundation for a new era of catalyst discovery
items.summary: Artificial intelligence is rapidly changing how scientists search for new catalysts—the materials that speed up chemical reactions essential for producing fuels, chemicals and clean energy technologies. However, despite remarkable advances in AI, a major obstacle remains: a lack of comprehensive, st

items.title: Scientists teach human cells to compute like tiny computers
items.summary: Researchers have developed a way to program human cells to perform calculations and make autonomous decisions, similar to how computer chips work.

items.title: New way to clean up environmental pollution using phage bioaugmentation

items.title: Study could unlock key to more reliable truffle cultivation
items.summary: As one of the world's most revered gourmet foods, some truffles can sell for more than €1,000 per kilogram, making cultivation a lucrative business. items.title: Nearby 'Super Earth' may be a better candidate for life than previously thought
items.summary: Using the Hobby-Eberly Telescope at McDonald Observatory, astronomers have taken a closer look at a nearby exoplanet and discovered it may be more Earth-like than previously thought. items.title: A cataclysmic collision in space provides new clues on astronomy's biggest stalemate
items.summary: Second only to black holes, neutron stars—incredibly dense star remnants—are the densest objects in the universe. When neutron stars collide, they create ripples in the fabric of space and time that we can detect on Earth.

items.title: Overconfident people struggle more to separate real headlines from false ones, study finds
items.summary: Confidence in specific judgments can predict resistance to misinformation, but a general tendency toward overconfidence might predict susceptibility to believing false claims.

items.title: Spatially explicit population model can improve pesticide risk assessments in agricultural landscapes
items.summary: A team of international scientists and risk assessment experts has developed a foundational blueprint for an innovative population model designed to improve environmental safety testing for agricultural pesticides. items.title: New research reveals the motivations and tactics used by call center fraudsters
items.summary: A new study led by the University of Portsmouth lifts the lid on the tactics used by call center fraudsters in India, while revealing the shocking scale of the industry within the country. items.title: AI can make a persuasive case for trans rights in short-term
items.summary: A chatbot can reduce prejudice against trans people—at least temporarily. Prior work has shown that these approaches are especially effective when paired with moral matching, or tailori

items.title: The invasive fern that science misidentified for decades
items.summary: Salvinia molesta can double its biomass in 36 hours. It spreads across ponds, lakes and slow-moving waterways in a smothering green mat, blocking sunlight, consuming oxygen and collapsing the ecosystems beneath it. Now present in freshwater bodies across more than 60 countries, it ranks among the to

items.title: Cultural frameworks may contribute to racial differences in parole decisions
items.summary: Parole candidates may be evaluated more favorably when they discuss their crimes using cultural frames more common among White Americans than among Black, Hispanic and East Asian Americans, according to a study.

items.title: Tiny ancient fish fossil with preserved brain offers clues to early fish evolution
items.summary: Over 300 million years ago, a minnow-sized fish died and fell to the bottom of a prehistoric swamp near the village of Trawden, Lancashire, in northwest England. items.title: Shattered dreams on Canada Day: Broken immigration promises are based on false narratives
items.summary: With Canada Day almost upon us, some new Canadians will celebrate in nationwide citizenship ceremonies—but countless migrants are facing the reality that they may never reach that milestone.

items.title: Arabian Sea sediments reveal summer and winter monsoons shifted differently after last ice age
items.summary: High-resolution sediment analyses from the Arabian Sea reveal, for the first time, that summer and winter monsoons respond differently to global climate change. The study enhances understanding of past precipitation patterns and could help refine climate models for regions influenced by monsoons.

items.title: How to stay cool in a heat wave even without air conditioning
items.summary: Heat can be dangerous, but health experts say there are ways to manage the threat.

items.title: The largest digital camera ever built begins decade-long survey of the universe
items.summary: The largest digital camera ever built is starting to capture images of unseen corners of the universe.

items.title: Fireworks, heat and drought make this July 4th a recipe for wildfires
items.summary: Across the United States, the sky will be erupting with fireworks on July 4, 2026, and the days around it as America celebrates the 250th anniversary of its independence. Many cities will be hosting spectacular fireworks shows.

items.title: Houston power plant emerges as dominant source of cloud-forming aerosols
items.summary: Research by atmospheric scientists at UC San Diego's Scripps Institution of Oceanography and colleagues pinpointed an individual coal-fired power plant in Houston as the main source of particles most likely to encourage the formation of clouds around the metropolitan area.

items.title: Physicists demonstrate Hong–Ou–Mandel interference with more than 10 atoms
items.summary: In a new study published in Nature Physics, researchers have demonstrated the Hong–Ou–Mandel (HOM) effect with up to 12 indistinguishable neutral atoms—an effect that has been predominantly observed in photonic systems.

items.title: A Most “Pleasantly Situated” Estate
items.summary: The farms and gardens of Mount Vernon played a central role in the life of America’s first president.

items.title: Adversity can follow NZ kids to the classroom. Can schools make a difference?
items.summary: By their eighth birthday, an estimated 9 in 10 New Zealand children will have experienced some form of serious adversity. They might have been neglected, grown up with family violence, lived through a separation or coped with a parent's mental illness or substance use problem.

items.title: Human activity has not always harmed biodiversity—quite the opposite
items.summary: For millennia, farming in Switzerland did not reduce plant diversity but helped increase it, University of Basel researchers have shown in a detailed reconstruction covering the past 7,000 years. Only recent decades paint a different picture.
